exits.label: ▾ down the stair, to the Underbridge
▴ up into the Lamplit Quarter (the Lower City)

npcs.name: Mother Sable
npcs.title: the Bone-Charm Seller
npcs.hail: Charms against the Wall, dearie — a knuckle of saint, a Kelemvor's-penny…

npcs.name: Sergeant Bram Holloway
npcs.title: Recruiter, the Ashen Watch
npcs.hail: Sign on with the Ashen Watch! Steady pay, hot meals, a cause worth—

npcs.name: Pip
npcs.title: a street child
npcs.hail: *(a small figure drifts a little too close to your purse…)*

npcs.name: Brother Calix
npcs.title: a Doomguide of Kelemvor
npcs.hail: Kelemvor keep you, traveler. The Judge weighs all souls fairly — *(a flicker)* — they tell us.

npcs.name: Wren
npcs.title: a woman buying winter apples
npcs.hail: *(a young woman counts coppers over a barrel of apples, smiling too hard at the seller)*

npcs.name: Goodman Tallow
npcs.title: a man who likes to watch
npcs.hail: *(a pleasant, unremarkable man leans by the wall, watching the crowd, and smiles when he catches your eye)*

npcs.name: Mad Joss
npcs.title: the Fountain-Prophet
npcs.hail: *(a wild-eyed soul in rags talks urgently to the fountain-water, and then, suddenly, to you)*

variants.text: *(The old charm-seller's milky eyes find you and *sharpen*, the way a merchant's do at a particularly promising customer.)* Ahh. *You.* *(she leans across her stall of knucklebones and knotted thread)* No god's light on you at all, godless one. Which means *you*, of everyone who's wandered past today, actually *need* what old Sable sells. The Wall's already got your name on a list, dearie. Shall we get it crossed off?

*(She clocks the grey balance-sigil at your throat and her sales-smile turns wry.)* A servant of the Judge. *(she snorts)* You don't need a single thing on this table, and we both know it — your soul's got a *reserved seat* downstairs. *(she winks)* But you'll buy a charm anyway. For someone you love who *isn't* as safe as you. Everyone like you does.

*(Her hand darts out and *covers* a tray of charms protectively the instant she sees your mark.)* …Put that away near my stall, would you. *(sourly)* The Bone Lord's sigil curdles my mourning-knots and sours the milk-charms. *(she eyes you)* What's the Lord of the Dead-and-Gone want with a poor woman's trinkets? Browsing, or *cursing*?

*(She takes in your horns — your skin — the things the market's other sellers flinched at, and instead she just nods, businesslike.)* Hells-blood, eh. Or the deep dark. *(she shrugs)* Makes no difference to old Sable — your coin spends the same, and your soul needs warding the same. *(she spreads her hands over the table)* Maybe *more*. What'll it be?

*(A bent old woman presides over a table of knucklebones, knotted thread, and dull copper discs, her voice a practiced sing-song.)* Charms against the Wall, traveler! A knuckle of saint to weigh your soul down sweet, a Kelemvor's-penny for the ferry, a knot of *true* mourning so someone, somewhere, grieves you proper. *(she taps the table)* A copper apiece keeps the nothing off. What'll you have?

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(She waits, gnarled hands folded, the patient stillness of a woman who has out-haggled three generations of this market.)* Well, dearie? Old Sable hasn't got all the daylight left — and neither, between us, have you.

choices.text: [INSIGHT] "These 'saint knuckles' are chicken bones, Mother. That 'Kelemvor's-penny' is a filed-down trade copper. None of this works, does it."
[RELIGION] "A true Kelemvor's-penny is struck on the dark of the moon and never shines. These are mint-bright. You're selling the *idea* of the rite, not the rite."
[FAITHLESS] "Don't sell me the chicken bone. Sell me the one that *actually* works on the godless — if it exists. And don't lie. I'm the Wall's already; I've nothing left to lose by knowing."
[CLERIC] "One grave-tender to another, Mother — what do you sell the people I can't help? The ones with no penny and no god?"
[PERSUASION] "A copper's steep for a chicken bone, Mother. Two charms for one copper, and I'll tell every godless soul on the causeway you're the honest one."
*(let her see, for one blink, the Wall's chill behind your eyes)* "You sell warding against the Wall, Mother. I have *been* to the Wall. I came back. Ask me what actually keeps the nothing off."
"Give me a knot of true mourning. Here's your copper."
"Not today, Mother." *(move on)*

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(The sing-song stops dead. For the first time her milky eyes truly *focus*, and what she sees makes her sit back hard on her stool, one hand drifting to her own knotted charm — the real one, the one she never sells.)* …Oh. *(barely a breath)* Oh, you poor, *cold* thing. You're not lying. *(she studies you the way she's studied forty years of the dying, and finds something she's never seen.)* You went *in*. Into the stone. And something — *(she swallows)* — something pulled you back out with the Wall still *on* you. *(quietly, the merchant entirely gone)* Then you already know what I really sell, don't you. Not bone. Not penny. *(she lifts a plain grey thread)* I sell a soul the *certainty* that when it's gone, one other soul will hold a thread and *remember* it was here. That's the only thing the nothing can't eat: a *witness.* *(she presses the thread into your dead-cold hand, and does not flinch at the cold.)* You came back to be ours, didn't you. The witness. The one who remembers the Faithless when no god will. *(she closes your fingers over it.)* Take it for free, Returned. And when you walk back toward that Wall — and you will — you carry every name this market never got to keep.

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(For a heartbeat her face goes flat and dangerous — and then it *cracks* into the first real smile you've seen in this market, delighted.)* …Ha! *(she slaps the table)* Chicken bones and trade coppers, every one. *(she lowers her voice, conspiratorial, *pleased*)* Forty years I've sold the *comfort*, dearie, not the bone. A frightened soul that *believes* it's warded walks to the Wall calmer, kinder, less alone — and that calm's the only true charm there ever was. *(she presses a knotted thread into your hand, free)* Take that one. It's just thread. But you'll think of being *seen* through, just now, and that's worth more than any saint's knuckle. Go on. No charge for the one who saw me.

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(Her sing-song curdles and she sweeps a protective arm across her wares.)* *Chicken bones.* *(coldly)* You come to a poor woman's table and call her a fraud on a *guess*, with no eye for the rite and no coin in your hand. *(she flaps a hand)* Off with you. The charms work for them as *believe*, and that was never going to be *you*. *(she turns pointedly to the next passerby — and that's the end of Sable's goodwill today.)*

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(The patter drops away entirely. She looks at you a long moment, then reaches *under* the table and sets down a single plain river-stone, smooth and grey.)* …Honest, then. All right. Honest. *(quietly)* There's no charm that keeps the Wall off the godless, dearie. There never was. *(she taps the stone)* But *this* — this is a holding-stone. You give it to someone living, and you tell them: *when I'm gone and unworshipped and they say I was nothing, hold this, and remember I was something to you.* *(she meets your eyes)* That's all any of us get against the nothing. Not a god. A *someone who remembers.* The stone's free. The remembering, you arrange yourself. *(gentler)* It's the only thing on this table that's real, and I only sell it to the ones who'd know the difference.

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(She softens, the merchant giving way to something older and tireder.)* The ones you can't help. *(she nods slowly)* Aye, those are *my* trade, padre — the no-penny, no-god poor your church files under *regrettable.* *(she lifts a knot of grey thread)* I sell them *this*, and a lie that it wards the Wall. *(she shrugs, unashamed)* You'd call it false comfort. I call it the only comfort that's *in stock* for folk your Judge won't seat. *(she presses the knot into your hand)* Take one to the next soul your doctrine fails. Tell them it's blessed. It isn't. But *you* holding it out to them — *that* part's true, and that's the part that works.

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(She cackles, genuinely tickled.)* *Two* for one, and free advertising! *(she counts out two knotted threads)* You drive a thief's bargain, dearie, and you flatter an old woman besides — that's the whole craft, right there, and you've got it. *(she winks)* Two mourning-knots. Tell your godless friends Sable's the honest fraud on the causeway. It's even *true*, mostly. Off you go, and mind the pickpocket by the crates — little Pip's quicker than you'd credit.

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(She's unmoved, the stone wall every market-haggler eventually hits.)* A copper apiece, dearie, and not a chip less. *(she folds her hands)* I've turned away smoother tongues than yours before noon. *(a flicker of warmth)* But you tried honest, and you didn't call me a fraud, so — here. *(she gives you a single knot anyway)* One mourning-knot, full price, and good fortune on the causeway. The Wall's patient. So am I.

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(✦ Something in your voice, your timing, the exact tilt of your head, achieves a kind of *perfection.* The old woman's jaw drifts open. A passing pigeon stops to watch.)* …Oh. *(faintly.)* Oh, you're *good.* *(she sits down hard on her stool, fanning herself with a saint's knuckle.)* Forty-one years I've worked this table and I have *never* — *(she shoves the entire tray at you)* — take them. Take all of them. The knots, the pennies, the chicken bones, take the *good* basket, the one under the table I don't show people. *(she's half-laughing, half-weeping, thoroughly undone.)* I'd give you the *stool* but I need to sit on something while I reconsider my entire life. *(she grabs your sleeve.)* Are you spoken for? Do you need a *grandmother?* I have a spare room and forty years of charm-craft and *no one to leave it to.* *(she presses a final knot into your hand, dazed and beaming.)* Go on. Go on before I make it weird. Weirder.

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(💀 You go for the disarming wink. It does not disarm. Something in the delivery — the eyebrow, perhaps, or the way your voice cracks on 'honest' — lands so catastrophically wrong that the old woman recoils as from a snake.)* …Are you having a *fit?* *(she's up off her stool, two charms thrust out like garlic at a vampire.)* Don't you — *don't you try your *patter* on old Sable, you oily — that was the worst — *(she's genuinely rattled now, and a small crowd is forming)* — I have sold *comfort* to the *terrified* for forty-one years and that is the most *threatening* offer of a *discount* I have ever heard in my LIFE. *(she flings a pinch of dried something at you that may or may not be a curse and is definitely chicken.)* Begone! Two coppers apiece for YOU now, and I hope your tongue *cramps!*

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(She takes your copper with the speed of long practice and presses the grey knot into your palm.)* A knot of true mourning. *(she pats your hand)* Now *someone* grieves you proper when the day comes, dearie — even if it's only this thread, and only because you paid for it. *(a crooked smile)* Don't knock it. That's more than half the souls on that Wall ever got. Go safe.

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(She's already looking past you to the next mark.)* Suit yourself, dearie. *(a parting sing-song)* The charms'll be here when the Wall starts feeling closer — and it always does, for everyone, in the end. *(she raises her voice to the crowd)* Charms against the Wall! A copper keeps the nothing off!

variants.text: *(The broad sergeant's recruiting-grin turns genuine the moment he reads how you carry your weight.)* Now *there's* a sword-arm. *(he straightens off his stall)* I've watched a hundred farm-boys try to *look* like they could hold a line today, friend, and you're the first who actually *could.* The Ashen Watch needs you. *(a beat, the grin tightening)* Gods, the Watch *needs* you. Sign on?

*(He marks the way you stand — back to the stall-post, eyes already counting the market's exits — and his pitch dies in his throat, replaced by a soldier's nod to a soldier.)* …You've served. *(quietly)* I can always tell. You're standing a watch right now and you don't even know you're doing it. *(he lowers his voice off the sales-pitch)* So I won't sell you the hot-meals line. You know what a recruiting stall this eager really means. Talk straight with me a minute?

*(His eyes travel up. And up.)* …Well now. *(he recovers his grin)* Built like a postern gate. Friend, the recruits follow a *back* like that without me saying a word — half of soldiering is who the scared lads line up behind, and they'd line up behind *you.* The Ashen Watch could use a wall that walks. Interested?

*(A barrel-chested man in ash-grey tabard hails the crowd from behind a stall hung with the Watch's banner.)* Sign on with the Ashen Watch! Steady pay, hot meals, and a cause worth—! *(his voice catches, just slightly, on the word)* —worth a great deal. *(he recovers, beaming at you)* You, friend! You've the look of someone the city could lean on. A moment of your time?

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(He plants both fists on the stall, the banner of the Ashen Watch sagging behind him in the still air.)* So. What'll it be? The Watch takes all comers with a strong back and a steady nerve — and pays in *actual coin*, which is more than most causes on this causeway manage.

choices.text: [STRENGTH] *(set your elbow on the stall)* "Arm-wrestle for it, Sergeant. I win, you tell me the *real* reason you're a recruiter short of recruits. You win, I'll hear your pitch out."
[INSIGHT] "You don't believe a word of your own pitch, Sergeant. Your voice breaks on 'a cause worth a great deal' every time. Where are you really sending these kids?"
[SOLDIER] "Which front, Sergeant? I've stood a few. The ones that need *this* many bodies *this* fast are never the ones in the recruiting song."
[INTIMIDATION] "Stop. Lying. To children. Pack up the stall, Sergeant, or I'll pack it up for you."

*(speak low, and let him hear the grave in your voice)* "The men you buried on that causeway are standing at your back right now, Sergeant. All three squads. They followed you home. They're not angry — they're *waiting* to see if you stop sending more."
"Where do I sign?"
"Not the soldiering life for me, Sergeant. Good fortune to you."

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(Every drop of colour leaves his broad face. The grin doesn't just fall — it *shatters.* His hand finds the stall-edge and grips it white.)* …How. *(a whisper, ragged)* How could you *know* that. I never — I've told *no one* about the three squads. Not the chaplain, not my wife. *(he turns, slowly, and looks at the empty air behind his own shoulder, where the banner hangs dead-still — and for one terrible second you watch a man almost, almost see them too.)* …Waiting. *(his voice breaks clean in half.)* You're saying they don't *blame* me. They're just — *watching* to see if I— *(he scrubs a fist hard across his eyes.)* I take down the banner. Today. Right now. Gods forgive me, I've sent forty lads up that causeway with a *song*, and the only ones who ever came back were the ones at my *back*, and I knew — I *knew* — *(he starts folding the ash-grey cloth with shaking hands.)* No more. Not one more. *(he looks at you, wrecked and somehow lighter.)* What *are* you, that you carry my dead for me and hand them back gentle? …No. Don't tell me. I don't think I could hold the answer. *(quietly)* Thank you, stranger. They can rest now. So, maybe, can I.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(He laughs — the first unguarded sound he's made all day — and clasps your hand.)* HA! A soldier's bargain. *(He sets his grip; he's strong, market-stall-strong, but you feel the difference and so does he. His arm goes down slow, then sudden.)* …Gods. *(he flexes his hand, grinning, beaten and glad of it)* All right. All *right.* A deal's a deal. *(the grin fades into something honest and tired)* The real reason. *(low)* It's the Wall, friend. The expedition's to the *Wall* — the church wants bodies to stand the causeway while their Doomguides do something up there they won't name. *(he meets your eyes)* The pay's real. The hot meals are real. The *coming back*… that part I can't promise, and I'm sick to my stomach of not saying so to the farm-boys. You beat the truth out of me fair. Do with it what a soldier does.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(The grin comes off like a helmet at the end of a long day. He glances at the farm-boys queued at the next stall over and lowers his voice.)* …You see clear. *(quietly)* Aye. I don't believe it. *(he exhales)* The 'cause' is the *Wall*, friend. They're massing bodies to stand the causeway while the church does something up there in the dark they won't put in the recruiting song. Pay's real. Meals are real. The *odds*… *(he shakes his head)* I've buried three squads I signed myself. *(he looks at you)* And every morning I stand this stall and lie sweet to children, because if *I* don't fill the quota, they send a recruiter who *won't* break on the word 'worth.' *(grim)* So now you know. Sign on with your eyes open, or talk me out of the next lad. Either's more than I deserve.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(The grin snaps back into place, a shade too fast.)* Believe it? Friend, I've *lived* it — twenty years in the ash-grey! *(he booms it for the queue's benefit)* A cause worth a great deal, and don't you let a cynic's squint tell you otherwise! *(but his eyes, just for a second, beg you to drop it)* Now — are you signing, or are you costing me my morning?

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(He doesn't bristle. He *deflates*, like a man who's been waiting for someone to say exactly that.)* …All right. *(quietly, he starts taking down the banner)* All right. You're right. *(he folds the ash-grey cloth with more care than it deserves)* I tell myself someone has to fill the quota, and better me, gentle, than the next one cruel. *(he meets your eyes)* But you're right. There's no gentle way to lie a child onto that causeway. *(he sets the folded banner down)* I'll stand down for the day. Maybe longer. Whatever they do to me for an empty quota, it's less than what I do to *them.* *(roughly)* …Thank you. I think. Now go, before I change my mind and my spine.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(Twenty years of parade-ground steel come up under the grin, and he does not budge an inch.)* *Mind* yourself. *(flat, hard)* I've held a line against worse than a loud stranger with opinions, and I'll hold this stall against you too. *(he squares up)* Threaten me again at my own post and we'll see which of us the city watch carries off. *(he jerks his chin)* Move along. The Watch doesn't recruit *bullies* — we've a full complement of those already, and they all outrank me.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(✦ You don't raise your voice. You don't have to. Whatever crosses your face in that instant is so absolute, so utterly without bluff, that twenty years of soldier in Bram Holloway recognizes a *superior officer* and snaps to attention before his brain catches up.)* SIR. *(he salutes. He doesn't know why. A small, dark patch appears on the front of his ash-grey trousers; neither of you will ever mention it.)* …I mean — *(he's already taking down the banner with shaking hands)* — yes, sir, packing up the stall, sir, immediately, sir. *(he knocks over his own charcoal pot.)* Permission to — to just go home, sir? Maybe forever? I think I'd like to go home forever. *(he pauses, banner half-folded, a wild new hope in his eyes.)* …You don't need a sergeant, do you? I've twenty years' experience and I will follow you into literally anything that *isn't that causeway.* I'll carry your bags. I'll carry *you.* Just — gods, *thank you* for making me stop.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(💀 You square up, you draw breath for the big menacing line — and at the precise apex of your threat your voice cracks, *spectacularly*, up into a register usually reserved for startled adolescents and small dogs, on the word 'pack.' A nearby pigeon takes flight. Someone, somewhere, laughs.)* *(Bram blinks. The grin returns, enormous and fond.)* …Aw. *(he reaches across the stall and pats your head.)* There, there. Big feelings, eh? Causeway gets us all worked up. *(he produces a slightly squashed barracks biscuit from a pocket and presses it on you.)* You have a sit-down, friend. Have a biscuit. *(to the gathering crowd, beaming)* Brave little soul, this one — came over all *fierce* about the children! Lungs on 'em! *(back to you, kindly)* Sign on when you've got your voice back, eh? We could use the *passion.* …and possibly a throat lozenge.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(He pushes the muster-roll and a charcoal stick across the stall, and his beam doesn't *quite* reach his eyes.)* Good lad. Good *lass.* Make your mark there. *(as you sign)* …Listen. *(lower, fast, like he can't help it)* Keep your eyes open up on the causeway, hear? Stand at the *back* of the formation, not the front. And if a Doomguide tells you to walk out past the last torch — *(he stops himself)* …ah, never mind an old sergeant's nerves. Welcome to the Ashen Watch. Hot meal's at the barracks. *(he holds your gaze a beat too long)* Come back from it.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(Something behind his eyes eases — a recruiter quietly relieved to have one *fewer* name on his conscience.)* No? *(he nods, and means it)* No. Fair enough, friend. It's not a life for everyone, and between you and me—  *(he doesn't finish)* —good fortune to you. *(he turns back to the crowd, the grin reassembling)* Sign on with the Ashen Watch! Steady pay, hot meals…! *(softer, just to himself, as you go)* …a cause worth a great deal.

variants.text: *(The small figure sizes you up in a practiced half-second — and, to your surprise, *relaxes*, deciding you're the safe kind.)* …You've got a kind face. *(matter-of-fact, not flattering — appraising)* Kind faces give coppers and don't grab. *(a grubby hand, palm up, hopeful)* Spare one? I'll tell you something true for it. Truer than anything Sable's selling, anyhow.

*(The child flinches back before you've even spoken, reading something in you that makes the small body go tight and ready to bolt.)* —Didn't touch nothing! *(quick, shrill)* Wasn't even *near* your purse, wasn't, you can check— *(already edging toward the gap between the crates)* …you've got *mean* eyes, mister. Missus. Whatever. The kind that don't give coppers. The kind that *grab.*

*(The kid's eyes go wide — not afraid, *fascinated* — taking in your horns, your skin, the strangeness the grown-ups crossed the street to avoid.)* …*Whoa.* *(hushed, delighted)* You're like *me.* The kind grown-ups cross the road from. *(a crooked grin)* That's almost *nice*, that is. Nobody crosses the road from a *little* monster, but a big one? *(the small hand, which had been drifting toward your purse, pauses)* …I won't lift off you. Professional courtesy. Monsters don't rob monsters.

*(You turn, and catch a small grubby hand already halfway to your purse-strings. The child freezes — then produces a smile of pure, practiced innocence.)* …Hi. *(brightly)* Hello. I was just— *(the hand retreats, slow, as if it were never there)* —admiring your *belt.* Lovely belt. Very… belt-shaped. *(a beat)* You didn't see anything, did you, friend? Course you didn't.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(The child hovers at the gap between two crates — close enough to talk, close enough to *run* — weighing you with the fast, tired arithmetic of someone who has learned that grown-ups are mostly a problem to be managed.)* So? *(wary)* What's it gonna be, then. You the grabbing kind, the preaching kind, or the *boring* kind that just walks off?

choices.text: [PERCEPTION] "Your other hand, Pip. The one behind your back. Open it — slowly — and let's see whose copper that is."
[INSIGHT] "Kids don't pick pockets for fun. You're *fast* and you're *scared* — which means it's not for you. Who are you stealing for, Pip?"
[DECEPTION] "Relax. I'm in the trade too — worked these stalls before you were born. Show me your patch and I'll show you a trick worth two purses."
[GOOD] *(crouch to her level; hold out a copper)* "Here. No tricks, no sermon. Just a copper, and you don't have to tell me anything you don't want to."

*(you've been gentle once already today, and it shows; the kid clocks it the way only the hunted can)* "You don't have to run from me, Pip. I'm the kind that helped someone else in this market not an hour ago. Word travels. Talk to me."

*(you feel the second, guttering little flame before you've decided to)* "There's another light near yours, Pip — small, and low to the ground, and going *out.* Your Wick is sicker than you've let yourself believe. I can feel it from here. Let me help, before there's nothing left to help."
[INTIMIDATION] "Hand. Purse. *Now*, you little gutter-rat, before I call the Watch."
"Keep your hands where I can see them and we'll get along. Carry on, Pip." *(move on)*

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(The child goes utterly, dangerously still — the stillness of a small animal that has just understood the thing in front of it is not another animal at all.)* …How do you— *(her voice comes out tiny)* Nobody knows about Wick. *Nobody.* I keep him *hid*, I keep him *safe*, I— *(her eyes are huge and wet and furious)* —you're not right, are you. You're one of the *cold* ones. The dead-that-walk. *(she doesn't run. She steps *closer*, fists balled, the bravest terrible thing you've seen all day.)* If you can *feel* him going out… *(her whole defiance cracks)* …then it's *true*, the thing I won't let myself— he's not getting *better*, is he. The cough. The grey lips. *(she grabs your dead-cold sleeve with both filthy hands.)* You're a *monster*, and you're the first grown-up who ever told me the *truth*, and I don't — I don't know how to do this without— *(she's crying now, openly, a child again at last.)* Help him. Please. I don't care what you are. *Help my brother.*

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(The small hand comes out from behind the back, and there's your copper, pinched between two filthy fingers. The kid stares at it like it betrayed her.)* …*Traitor coin.* *(she sighs, the whole performance collapsing)* Fine. *Fine.* You got me. *(she doesn't run — that surprises you)* You gonna call the Watch now? *(a tired shrug, far too old for the small face)* Go on, then. The lockup's warmer than the crates, and they feed you Tendays. Worst threat you've got is a *roof.* *(she flips the copper back to you, glum)* Quickest hands on the causeway and I get caught by somebody who actually *looks.* Nobody looks. That's the whole job.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(She waits, the stolen copper back in your palm, braced for whichever kind of grown-up you turn out to be.)*

choices.text: [INSIGHT] "You didn't run. You're not stealing for yourself — you'd have bolted to protect the haul. Who eats tonight if you don't lift my purse, Pip?"
*(flip the copper back to her)* "Keep it. And the next one's yours too if you tell me straight who you're really lifting for."
"Don't do it again where I can see. Off you go."

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(The child opens both hands wide, palms empty, the very picture of wounded innocence — and your purse, you notice, is still on your belt after all.)* See? *(reproachful)* *Empty.* Both of 'em. You're as bad as the Watch, seeing thieves in every shadow. *(she sniffs)* Maybe I was just standing here. Maybe the crates are a *nice place to stand.* *(but there's the ghost of a grin)* …You looked, though. Most don't. That's something.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(The practiced patter falls away, and for a second she just looks like what she is — a tired kid by some crates.)* …For Wick. *(quietly)* My brother. He's little. Littler than me, and he don't — he can't *run*, his leg's wrong, so he can't lift, so *I* lift, for both. *(fast, defensive)* I don't take from them as can't spare it! I watched you. You've got *spare.* That's the whole rule — only take from spare. *(she lifts her chin)* It's not *stealing* if they were gonna lose it on Sable's chicken bones anyway. It's just… *moving coppers* to where they're needed more. *(a beat, smaller)* Are you gonna tell the Watch about Wick? They take the little ones to the *workhouse.* Please don't tell about Wick.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(She watches your face, the whole of her small world balanced on what you do next.)*

choices.text: *(press a fistful of coppers into her hand)* "I won't tell anyone about Wick. Buy him something hot. And come find me if the crates stop being enough."
[PERSUASION] "There's an almshouse by the Kelemvor chapel that doesn't ask questions or split up kin. Mother Cass runs it. Tell her Pip-and-Wick, and tell her a stranger sent you."
"Your secret's safe. But the spare you took stays mine. Look after your brother, Pip."

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(Her eyes narrow, then *gleam* — a fellow professional, supposedly.)* …You're in the trade? *(she circles you once, appraising)* Huh. You've got the *patter*, I'll give you that. *(she sticks out a grubby hand to shake — and you feel her other hand ghost toward your purse even as she does it, testing you)* Partners, then. Show me this two-purse trick, *partner.* *(the grin is all teeth)* …'Course, if you were *lying* about being in the trade, that'd make you just another mark playing dress-up. And I don't show my patch to *marks.* So which is it?

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(The shutters come down. Whatever you reached for, you missed, and she's not giving it away for free.)* …Dunno what you mean. *(flat, practiced)* I'm just a kid by some crates. *(she pockets her hands)* You're the *preaching* kind, I reckon. Ask a lot of questions, give nothing, walk off feeling clever. *(she edges back)* Go on, then. Go feel clever somewhere else. The crates were here first.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(She takes a step back, the offer landing wrong on a kid who's heard a hundred grown-up promises.)* …An almshouse. *(suspicious)* That's what they *all* say, mister. 'A nice place, doesn't split you up.' And then it's the workhouse and they put Wick on one cart and me on another and that's the *last I ever—* *(she stops, breathing hard)* …No. No, I look after Wick. *Me.* Not your Mother Cass. *(she's already backing into the crate-gap)* …but. *(a flicker)* …if it gets bad. Maybe. I'll *remember* the name. That's all. Don't follow me.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(She stares at the coppers in her hand, then at you, with the open, undone astonishment of a child who genuinely did not know this was a thing grown-ups did.)* …Oh. *(very quietly)* You — you just *gave* it. No trick. No sermon. *(she closes her fist around the coins like they might escape)* People don't. People *don't.* *(she scrubs roughly at one eye with the back of her wrist and pretends she didn't)* …Wick's gonna eat *hot* tonight. With *gravy.* *(a fierce, wobbling grin)* I'm not gonna lift off you. Not ever. You're on the list — the *good* list, the one that's just got you on it now. *(she's already darting toward the crates, then turns back)* …If you're ever stuck on the causeway, you find a kid and you say *'Pip says we're square.'* They'll help you. We *all* help the good-list ones. *(and she's gone, quick as a sparrow, richer by a few coppers and the rarer thing besides.)*

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(There's no skill-check to this one. The word 'Watch' does what a closed fist couldn't — the child goes white and bolts, a streak of grubby terror vanishing between the crates before the threat's even finished leaving your mouth.)* *(Somewhere in the gap between the barrels, a small voice, shaking:)* —wasn't even gonna take much— *(and then nothing. The coppers Pip didn't get stay in your purse. The thing you could have learned about why a child works this market alone goes with her.)*

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(✦ You loom. You menace. You deliver 'gutter-rat' with such withering, theatrical villainy that the child's terror curdles, halfway through the flinch, into something far more dangerous: pure, undiluted *admiration.*)* …Whoa. *(she does not run. She steps *closer*, eyes shining.)* That was the *coolest* thing I ever saw. *(awed.)* The voice, an' the — *(she does an impression of your loom, badly)* — *Hand. Purse. NOW.* Can you *teach* me that? I been working this market two years and I never once made nobody piss themselves a *little*, and you just— *(she's bouncing.)* Are you a *villain?* Like a real one? Do you have a *lair?* *(she sticks out a filthy hand.)* I'm Pip. I'm gonna be your apprentice now. I already decided. You can't stop me, I'm very fast. *(beaming up at you, your terrifying threat entirely repurposed as a career opportunity.)* So what're we *robbing?*

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(💀 You draw yourself up to your most fearsome height to deliver the line — and your foot finds the one wet cobble in the entire square. There is a moment of windmilling. There is a dignity-ending little hop. You do not *quite* fall, which is somehow worse, and 'gutter-rat' comes out as a startled 'gutter-WHOOP.')* *(Pip stares. Then Pip *loses it.*)* *(she's doubled over the crates, wheezing.)* You— *(gasp)* —you said *gutter-whoop* — *(she's crying)* — an' then you did the little *dance* — *(she can't breathe)* — oh that's the funniest thing, that's the funniest thing that ever happened to me, I'm gonna think about that *forever.* *(she wipes her eyes, grinning enormously, entirely un-intimidated.)* Tell you what — for that? You can keep your purse. *Worth* it. *(she's still giggling.)* Mind the cobble on the way out, *gutter-whoop.* Ha! Gutter-whoop.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(The child relaxes a fraction — a grown-up who isn't a problem after all.)* …Right. *(a nod, almost respectful)* The boring kind. *(but not unkindly)* That's the best kind, mostly. *(she settles back against the crates, watchful)* Mind how you go. And mind your purse near the fountain — there's *worse* than me works this square, and they don't give the belt-admiring speech first. *(a quick grin)* That one's free. Professional courtesy.

variants.text: *(He brightens to see a fellow servant of the Judge — and then, strangely, dims, as if a shared faith makes the thing on his mind *harder* to carry, not easier.)* A brother in the grey. *(he touches his balance-sigil, then can't meet your eyes.)* Then you'll understand why I— *(he stops himself, straightens a candle.)* ...Kelemvor keep you. How fares the Judge's work, where you walk it? Mine fares... badly. If I'm honest. And I am trying, lately, to be honest.

*(His eyes widen — a Doomguide should not be *glad* to see one of the godless, and the gladness frightens him more than your lack of a god does.)* You carry no god's light at all. *(he lowers his voice, glancing toward the Justiciars' banner.)* I should warn you off this square. I should do a great many things. *(quietly, almost pleading)* Instead I find I want to *ask* you something no faithful soul could answer. Stay. Please. Just a moment of a doubting man's time.

*(He goes pale, his hand finding his holy symbol — and then, instead of an alarm, he just looks unutterably tired.)* The Bone Lord's mark. Here, at a shrine of the god who unseated him. *(he exhales.)* I should call the Justiciars down on you. *(he doesn't move.)* I haven't the heart for one more soul on the Wall today, not even yours. Move along, before I find it again.

*(The old church-taught flinch crosses his face the instant he sees your blood — and he is visibly, miserably ashamed of it.)* ...Forgive me. *(he makes himself look at you properly.)* I was taught from the cradle to see the Hells in a face like yours, and I am unlearning it. Slowly. Badly. A candle at a time. *(steadier)* Kelemvor keep you, whatever runs in your veins. What do you need of a poor shrine-cart?

*(He watches how the market *moves* around you — the desperate not flinching, the small kindnesses trailing in your wake like a wake of light — and something in his ruined faith steadies just to see it.)* ...You're a *gentle* one. *(quietly, wonderingly.)* I can tell. The square's been talking — the soul who *helps.* *(he sets down a candle, and for the first time the worn blessing leaves his face and something real arrives.)* Do you know, watching you cross this market is the first thing in a year that's made me think mercy might not be a *heresy* after all. *(he meets your eyes, almost pleading.)* How do you *do* it? Stay kind, in a place built to grind the kindness out of you? Tell me. I think my whole faith might hang on the answer.

*(He's heard the other thing the market whispers — the hard hand, the cowed, the frightened — and a young man already losing his faith looks at you with a careful, sorrowful wariness.)* ...Kelemvor keep you. *(he doesn't quite manage the blessing's warmth.)* They say you've a *hard* way about you, traveler. I won't pretend that doesn't trouble a man whose whole creed is *judgment.* *(but he steadies, and something desperate surfaces.)* And yet — you of *all* the souls in this square might be the one who can answer me honestly. You who don't flinch from hard things. *Is* cruelty ever truly just? The Wall says yes. I am beginning to think it lies. Tell me which of us is right, before I lose the nerve to ask.

*(A young Doomguide tends a small cart of grey mourning-candles, his vestments crisp and his face the exact opposite. He musters a blessing that has clearly cost him something today.)* Kelemvor keep you, traveler. The Judge weighs every soul fairly. *(the words come out worn smooth as a stair, and something underneath them wavers badly.)* ...Is there something you need? A candle, for your dead? We've grey ones, for the kind no one else will mourn.

nodes.speaker: Brother Calix
nodes.text: *(He straightens a row of candles that did not need straightening, then gives up the pretense entirely.)* Forgive me. I'm poor company. *(it comes out of him like water through a cracked dam.)* They send us to the causeway, you understand. To stand the Wall of the Faithless. To *watch the godless go in.* And I was taught it was just — they served no god, they *chose* the nothing, the law is the law and the law is mercy. *(his voice tightens to breaking.)* But I have begun to *see* them, up close, at the end. And not one of them — not *one* — looks like a soul that chose anything at all. They look like people who were simply... *missed.* Overlooked. *(he meets your eyes, desperate.)* Tell me I'm wrong, traveler. Someone. Please. Tell me the doctrine is right and let me sleep.

choices.text: [RELIGION] "You're not wrong, Brother — and the canon knows it. Kelemvor himself wanted the Wall gone and could not manage it. You serve a god who *hates* the thing you guard. That isn't heresy. That's just reading your own doctrine honestly."

[INSIGHT] "You're not asking whether the doctrine is right, Brother. You already know it isn't. You're asking whether you're *allowed* to act on knowing — whether a good man can disobey a holy order. Aren't you."

[KELEMVOR] "By the Judge we both serve — He took the Throne of Bones to make the dead's lot *kinder*, Brother. Standing the Wall was never His work. *Reforming* it is. Be the Doomguide He actually needs, not the one the Choir wants."
[FAITHLESS] "I'm one of the missed, Brother. Godless. Bound for your Wall the day I die. *Look* at me — and tell me, to my face, what it is I *chose.*"
[HERETICAL] "You and I have already burned a doctrine or two today, haven't we. Here's one more for the fire: a god who *needs* a Wall isn't a god worth the grey you're wearing. Take it off."

[PERSUASION] "Then stop standing the Wall. A man who can't bear his orders and obeys them anyway isn't *faithful*, Brother — he's only afraid. Walk off that causeway. Today. Let someone else's conscience fill the post."

*(let him feel the Wall's chill roll off you, cold as the causeway at dusk)* "I'll tell you what's on the far side, Brother. I've *been* there. And you've stood that Wall so long that a piece of you is already mortared into it — I can *see* it on you. Keep guarding it, and one day you won't have to die to join it."
"I can't answer that, Brother. I'm no theologian. I only know the candles are kind, and that's something."

nodes.speaker: Brother Calix
nodes.text: *(He stares at you as a man stares at a door he's been leaning against for years, suddenly opened.)* ...He wanted it *gone.* *(slowly.)* They teach us that — Kelemvor's mercy, Kelemvor's reform — and then in the very next breath they teach us to *stand the Wall*, and I never once put the two beside each other and *looked.* *(a terrible, freeing laugh.)* I've been guarding the one thing my god is ashamed of, and calling it devotion. *(he grips the cart's edge.)* You haven't given me doubt, traveler. I had doubt to spare. You've given me the worse thing. *Permission.* Now I have to *do* something with it, and gods, that's so much harder. *(he meets your eyes, steadier than he's been all day.)* Thank you. I think you've just cost me my post. It was a bad post.

nodes.speaker: Brother Calix
nodes.text: *(He flinches as if you'd touched a wound, because you have.)* ...Allowed. *(barely.)* Yes. That's it exactly. I know what's true. I've known for a year. What I don't know is whether a small man is *permitted* to set his own conscience against the whole weight of his church and his god and his order. *(he looks at his own hands.)* Cowards always make it a question of permission, don't we. So that someone else stays responsible. *(he straightens, and something settles in him.)* No one's going to *give* me permission, are they. That's not how it works. I just have to *take* it, and carry what comes. *(quietly.)* You've a cruel kind of kindness, traveler. You've left me nowhere to hide. Kelemvor keep you. I've a decision to go and make.

nodes.speaker: Brother Calix
nodes.text: *(Hope and fear war across his young face, and hope — barely — wins.)* Reform it. *(he breathes it like a heresy and a prayer at once.)* Not desert. Not run. *Stay*, and rot the rot from inside, and be the Doomguide the Judge actually wanted. *(he grips his sigil.)* They'll hate me for it. The Choir will call me faithless in all but the mark. But you're *right* — I took these vows to the *god*, not to the men who profit from His Wall, and the god and the Wall do not want the same thing. *(he stands taller than his years.)* I'll stand the causeway still. But I'll stand it *differently.* I'll learn the names. I'll write them down. I'll make the church *look* at what it files away. One Doomguide, refusing to forget. *(fiercely grateful.)* That's a post worth keeping. Thank you, brother. You've turned my despair into *work*, and work I can survive.

nodes.speaker: Brother Calix
nodes.text: *(He looks at you — really looks, the way his training forbids — and what he finds undoes him.)* ...Nothing. *(a whisper.)* You chose *nothing.* You're not wicked. You're not defiant. You're just a person who never happened to kneel, and for *that* my church has reserved you a place in a wall of screaming stone forever. *(his eyes fill.)* I have blessed that Wall. I have *stood* it. And you are standing in front of me being *kind to a doubting stranger* with the death-sentence already on you, and I— *(he wipes his face roughly.)* I cannot serve a doctrine that does this to *you.* I won't. *(he presses a grey candle into your hand.)* Take this. It's the mourning-grey, for the unmourned. When the Wall takes you — and gods forgive my church, it will — light it, and know that *one* Doomguide grieved you on purpose, in advance, in defiance of every order. It's all I have. It isn't enough. Nothing is.

nodes.speaker: Brother Calix
nodes.text: *(He laughs — startled, then helpless, then with a wild relief.)* Take it *off.* *(he touches the grey vestment as if seeing it new.)* You say it like it's *simple.* *(and then, wonderingly:)* ...Is it simple? I've made it a cathedral of agony in my head, and you just— *(he shakes his head.)* You're a dangerous soul to leave a man alone with. *(but he's smiling, the first real one.)* I won't take it off. Not today. But you've shown me the grey is a *costume*, not a cage — that I can wear it and still betray everything it's supposed to mean, every single day, on the side of the dead. *(fierce, quiet.)* A heretic in Doomguide's grey, smuggling mercy past my own church. *(he salutes you, ridiculous and sincere.)* Best heresy I've heard all year. And I hear a lot of heresy lately. Mostly from myself.

nodes.speaker: Brother Calix
nodes.text: *(The word *afraid* lands like a slap, and he doesn't flinch from it.)* ...Afraid. *(quietly.)* Yes. That's the honest name for it, isn't it. Not faith. Fear, wearing faith's good clothes. *(he begins, slowly, to gather his candles into their box.)* I tell myself if *I* don't stand the Wall, a crueler man will — and that's even true, and it's *also* exactly the lie every gentle hand on a terrible machine has ever told itself. *(he closes the box.)* I can't unmake the Wall by leaving. But I can stop being one of the hands that *works* it. That's not nothing. That's the only thing that's actually mine to do. *(he meets your eyes.)* I'm walking off the causeway, traveler. Tonight. I don't know what a Doomguide is when he won't guard the dead's prison. I suppose I'll find out. Thank you — for not letting me hide behind 'someone has to.'

nodes.speaker: Brother Calix
nodes.text: *(He shakes his head, and there's a stubborn, frightened steel under the doubt.)* No. *(quietly.)* You make it sound brave, walking away. But if every soul who *sees* the wrong of it leaves, then only the ones who *don't* see it are left holding the causeway — and gods help the Faithless then. *(he straightens.)* I'm not ready to abandon them to crueler keepers just to clean my own conscience. *(a flicker of the old fear.)* Maybe that's another coward's excuse. Maybe it's the one true thing I've said today. I can't tell anymore. *(he musters the blessing again, frayed.)* Kelemvor keep you, traveler. I have a causeway to go and stand, and hate, and stand anyway. For now.

nodes.speaker: Brother Calix
nodes.text: *(✦ Your words don't argue with his doubt — they *consecrate* it. They land with the weight and rhythm of scripture, the real kind, the kind his seminary never quite managed, and the young Doomguide's face goes luminous with a terrible, joyful resolve.)* *(He reaches up and tears the grey vestment from his shoulders in one ringing motion—)* THEN I WILL NOT STAND IT! *(—and immediately, the autumn air finding his shirtsleeves, makes a small involuntary noise and puts the cloak part back on.)* …It's *cold.* But the *principle* stands! *(he's laughing, half-weeping, alight.)* You're right, you're *right* — I took my vows to the *god*, not to His jailers, and a coward in holy grey is still a *coward* — *(he kicks over his own candle-cart in his fervour, then guiltily rights it.)* I'm *done.* I'm walking off that causeway tonight and I'm taking every grey candle with me and I'm going to learn the names, *all* the names, and I am going to make this whole rotten church *choke* on them! *(he grips your hands, shining.)* You didn't give me doubt, friend. You gave me a *calling.* A *better* one. Gods keep you — no — *I'll* keep you. In my prayers. The new ones. The dangerous ones.

nodes.speaker: Brother Calix
nodes.text: *(💀 You build to a rousing crescendo about cowardice and conscience — and somewhere in the soaring rhetoric you reach for *too* grand a flourish, invoke duty and the watching dead and a soul's true calling, and the young Doomguide's wavering face firms up with exactly, *precisely* the wrong conviction.)* …You're right. *(he straightens, eyes shining with renewed purpose.)* You're *absolutely* right. *(oh no.)* A man who *abandons* his post out of *fear* is a coward — and gods help me, I have been *letting* my fear dress itself up as *doubt*, haven't I, *tempting* me to *desert* the very souls who need a kind keeper most! *(he seizes his sigil, restored.)* *Thank* you, traveler. You've reminded me what the grey is *for.* I shall stand that causeway with a *whole* heart now, and tend the Faithless with all the more devotion for having nearly *faltered* — *(he beams at you, radiant with the renewed faith you have, somehow, with a stirring speech against the Wall, accidentally *rebuilt.*)* Kelemvor *keep* you. You've saved a doubting man's soul today!

nodes.speaker: Brother Calix
nodes.text: *(He goes white to the lips. His hand rises halfway to his sigil and stops, because a holy symbol is no use against the thing he's just understood you are.)* ...You've *been* there. *(a whisper.)* Past the Wall. And *back.* *(he stares at you, and you watch six years of doctrine crack down the middle.)* Then it's *true* — what I feel on the causeway, the cold that gets *into* a man and doesn't leave. You're saying I'm — that standing the Wall is slowly making me *part* of it. *(he touches his own chest as if checking he's still warm.)* Gods. *Gods.* The doctrine never warned us of *that*, did it. That the keepers and the kept end the same. *(he meets your eyes, terrified and, underneath it, freed.)* I felt myself going cold and I called it *discipline.* You've named it. *(unsteady.)* I'd rather walk away alive than freeze into my own Wall a watch at a time. Thank you, Returned. I think you may have just pulled me out of the stone before it set.

nodes.speaker: Brother Calix
nodes.text: *(He accepts the gentleness for what it is, and some of the desperation eases.)* ...The candles are kind. *(a small, real smile.)* Yes. They are. That's why I keep them — when the doctrine gives me nothing, a grey candle for an unmourned soul is at least *something* my own two hands can do that's good. *(he straightens the row again, calmer now.)* Perhaps that's where a doubting man starts. Not with the whole Wall. Just with the one small kindness in front of him. *(he offers you a candle.)* Take one anyway. For your dead, or for the day you'll need it. Kelemvor keep you, traveler. You've been kinder to me than my own certainty ever was.

variants.text: *(She exhales the instant she sees you — the held breath of the entire morning finally let go — and her smile, for the first time, reaches all the way to her eyes.)* It was *you.* With the watcher. By the wall. I saw the whole thing — I don't know what you said to that smiling man but he *left*, he actually folded up his little notebook and *left.* *(her voice shakes.)* I don't even know your name. I'm Wren. *(wet, fierce.)* Thank you. Gods, *thank you.* You bought me a *day*, maybe more. You've no idea what a day is worth to someone who's been counting them.

*(She's gathering her things in jerky, terrified haste, and when she sees you her face does something awful — she *knows.*)* You. *(barely.)* The watcher said a stranger gave him my name. Plain hair, your face. *(she clutches the apples to her chest like a shield.)* Why. *Why* would you— I never did anything to you. I was just buying *apples.* *(backing away.)* Stay away from me. Whatever you are. Just — stay *away.*

*(Something in how you carry yourself — godless, unclaimed, the same weather she lives under — makes her go very still, then very warm, the recognition of one hunted thing for another.)* ...You're like me. *(barely a breath, her smile still aimed at the apple-seller for cover.)* No god's light on you either. *(she turns an apple over and over.)* Don't look at me like you *know*, please. He's watching. The smiling one, by the wall. He's been watching me for an hour. Just— buy an apple. Pretend we're talking about apples.

*(She's heard there's a *kind* one working the market today — the rare kind, the kind that helps and doesn't grab — and when she fits your face to the whisper, the held breath of the whole morning finally shudders out of her.)* Oh. *Oh*, it's *you*, isn't it. The gentle one. They've been — people have been *saying.* *(her smile cracks, and what's under it is desperate hope.)* I don't — I don't usually— but they say you're *safe.* That you *help.* *(barely.)* Please. Please be safe. I've run all the way out of people I can say this to.

*(She takes one look at how the market flinches in your wake — the hard set of you, the word the stall-keepers are passing along — and the cornered animal in her decides, fast, to be *very* uninteresting.)* …Just buying apples. *(she doesn't meet your eyes; the smile is nailed on tight.)* Nothing here for you. No trouble, no — nothing. Just a woman and her apples. *(she's already counting coppers with shaking hands, willing you to find someone else to *be* whatever you are at.)*

*(She glances at you, makes the fast, practiced judgment of the cornered, and decides you're safe enough to stand near.)* Oh — sorry, am I in your light? *(she shifts along the barrel, smiling, always smiling.)* They're good this year, the apples. Sweet. *(she says it as though it matters *enormously* that the apples are sweet — as though it might be the most important thing she has left to be sure of.)*

*(A young woman counts coppers over a barrel of winter apples, and her smile is a held breath wearing a face. She doesn't quite look at you.)* ...Help you? Oh — no. Sorry. You don't work here, do you. *(a small, brittle laugh.)* I'm only buying apples. For — for later. Just apples.

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(She picks an apple up, turns it over, sets it down, picks it up again — hands that need something, anything, to do.)* Lovely day. *(her eyes flick once, helplessly, toward a pleasant smiling man leaning by the far wall, and snap back.)* I really should finish up and get home. It's only apples. *(the smile fractures, the smallest hairline crack.)* ...You keep *looking* at me. Why do you keep looking at me like that?

choices.text: [INSIGHT] "Because you're not buying apples — you're standing in the most crowded corner you could find because crowds *feel* safe, and they aren't, and you know that too. Something is very wrong, and you've nobody to tell. Tell me."

[MEDICINE] "*(you know the signs)* The grey creeping up your wrists. The cold in your fingers in this full sun. That isn't a chill, Wren. That isn't illness. You've been *marked.* The harvest has your name written down."
[PERCEPTION] "There's a man by the wall who's watched you for an hour, and you've watched him back the whole time pretending not to. The smiling one with the little book. Who is he to you, Wren?"

[PERSUASION] "I'm not going to hurt you. I'd swear it on a god if I had one, and I don't, which is rather the point — I've nothing to gain from you and nowhere to report you. Trust the one stranger in this square with empty hands. What's happened?"

*(gently, terribly)* "I can see the mark on you, Wren. Like cold light, all over you. You have days — maybe less. And you already know; you've been saying goodbye to apples all morning. I'm sorry. I'm so sorry. But I came back from where you're going, and it is not the nothing they told you it is."
"Sorry. Didn't mean to stare. Good apples." *(move on)*

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(The apple goes still in her hands. When she speaks the smile is gone and what's under it is much younger and much older at once.)* ...Three days ago a man came to the door. *(very quietly.)* Pleasant. Apologetic, even. Said my name was on a list. Said I'd been *assessed* — godless, no temple-tithe, no patron — and that the harvest would 'see to me' before the tenday's out, and that I should 'set my affairs in order,' as if I had affairs. As if I had anything but a brother and a room and *apples.* *(her voice cracks clean through.)* And then he *tipped his hat* and left. There's no one to appeal to. There's no god to pray to — that's the whole *reason*, isn't it. *(she looks at you, drowning.)* So I'm buying apples. Because they're sweet, and I'm alive *today*, and I have decided to taste every single thing I can before — *(she can't finish.)* You asked. That's what's wrong. *Everything.* Everything is what's wrong.

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(Her hand flies to her wrist, covering the grey, and her eyes go huge.)* You can — you can *see* it? *(a whisper of pure terror.)* They said no one could see it but the harvesters. That it was *quiet.* That I could pretend, right up until— *(she steadies herself on the barrel.)* Three days. They gave me notice, like a *landlord.* Godless, unclaimed, *assessed.* My name on a list in an office I'll never see, signed by a man I'll never meet. *(fierce, shaking.)* And do you know the worst of it? I keep thinking I must have *done* something. I lie awake hunting for the sin. But there isn't one. That's the part no one will say out loud. *I didn't do anything.* I just never knelt to anyone, and for that they're going to fold me into a wall. *(she looks at you.)* You see it. So you know I'm not mad. That's — gods, that's almost a *comfort.* Someone else can see the cold.

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(She doesn't look at him this time. She looks at her hands.)* ...His name's Tallow, I think. Goodman Tallow. *(barely.)* He's not Watch, not church, not anyone you could *report.* He just... *notices.* He stands in markets and he watches for the ones with no god's light, and he writes down names, and the names go to the Choir, and then the men come to the doors. *(her knuckles whiten.)* He's not even *certain* of me yet. That's why he's still watching instead of gone. He likes to be *thorough.* *(she finally looks up, and it's worse than tears.)* If he were sure, I'd already be a name on a list instead of a woman buying apples. So I stand here. And I smile. And I let him be *unsure* for one more hour. It's all the defense I have. Being not-quite-worth-mentioning.

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(She searches your face for the catch, the angle, the way everyone's kindness has had an angle lately — and doesn't find one, and the not-finding nearly breaks her.)* Empty hands. *(unsteadily.)* You really haven't anything to gain, have you. That's so *rare* I'd forgotten what it looked like. *(she lets a breath out.)* All right. The truth, then, fast, before the smiling man drifts closer: I'm marked. The harvest. Three days' notice, godless and unclaimed, and there's nowhere to run that the list doesn't reach and no god to hide behind because *not having one is the crime.* *(she grips your sleeve, suddenly, fiercely.)* But you've empty hands and a steady face and you can see I'm not mad. So I'm going to do the stupidest, most desperate thing a cornered woman can do. I'm going to *ask a stranger for help.* Is there anything — *anything* — you can do? Or even just— tell me what you'd do, if it were you.

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(The shutters come down. She's been promised safety by too many smiling mouths this week.)* ...That's kind of you to say. *(flatly, the social smile snapping back into place like armor.)* But I don't know you, and the last few strangers who took an interest in me were taking an *interest*, if you follow. *(she gathers her apples.)* So I'll just finish my shopping, thank you, and you'll go on your way, and we'll both pretend this was about *apples.* *(quieter, not unkind.)* It's not you. It's that I've run out of trust the way you run out of bread. There's just none left in the jar. Good day.

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(✦ Something about *empty hands* — the way you say it, the way you actually *show* them, palms up, nothing hidden — cracks a dam that's been holding back a whole flood of a week, and Wren trusts you so completely, so instantly and totally, that it nearly knocks you both over.)* Oh thank the — *(she grabs both your empty hands in both of hers.)* You're *real.* You're actually *real* and *safe* and you don't *want* anything — *(it all comes out at once, a torrent:)* I'm marked, harvest, three days, godless, there's a watcher called Tallow by the wall, my landlady already re-let my room, I haven't *slept*, I keep a knife in my boot but I don't know how to *use* it, my mother's name was Anwen and I'm named for the bird because she said I'd fly, and *isn't that funny now*, and I have *no one*, I have had *no one* for three days, and you have *kind eyes* and *empty hands* and— *(she stops, breathless, having told you roughly four secrets more than strictly necessary.)* …I don't even know your name. You'll be the godparent. Of my children. If I— if there are ever— *(she laughs, wet and slightly hysterical.)* Sorry. Sorry. It's just been a *very* long week to do entirely *alone.* Help me. Please. I'll never trust anyone this fast again, so it had better be *you.*

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(💀 You mean it with your whole heart. That is the *problem.* 'I'm not going to hurt you' comes out of you with such sudden, fervent, white-knuckled *intensity* — you lean in, your voice drops to a passionate growl, you may have said the words 'with my LIFE' and possibly 'and my TEETH' — that the cornered woman's eyes go very wide and she takes a careful step back into the apple barrel.)* …Right. *(faintly.)* Yes. Thank you. That's — that's a *lot* of feelings about a stranger's *safety.* *(she's gripping a winter apple like a weapon now.)* The — the *teeth* part especially. *(she edges sideways.)* You know what, I think I'm — I think I'll be fine, actually, I've just remembered I'm very late for a thing, a normal thing, with *people*, lots of people, *witnesses* — *(she's leaving, fast, smiling the terrified social smile.)* You take care now! Maybe fewer — fewer teeth, with the next one! *(and she's gone, and you have managed, through sheer overwhelming sincerity, to become the *second* most frightening thing in her week.)*

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(For one long moment she does not breathe at all. The apple rolls from her hand and neither of you moves to catch it.)* ...You can *see* it. *(a whisper with nothing left under it.)* Not guessing. *Seeing.* The cold light. *(her eyes fill, but her voice goes strangely steady, the steadiness of someone who's just been handed the truth they've been begging and dreading.)* Days. You said days. *(she nods, slowly, as though filing it.)* I knew. I think I knew. But everyone kept *smiling* at me and saying *you look well, Wren*, and I started to think maybe I was imagining the cold, maybe I could just— apple my way out of it. *(she wipes her face once, fiercely.)* And you say it isn't the nothing they promised. The Wall. You'd know. You came *back.* *(she grips your arm with both hands.)* Then tell me — quickly, before the watcher drifts over — tell me what's actually *there*, so I can stop being so afraid of the wrong thing. Please. You're the only one who can. The only one who'd *know.*

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(She waits, the whole fragile remainder of her balanced on what a stranger does next.)*

choices.text: *(low and fast)* "Don't go home. Home is the first place the list sends them. There are people who run the marked out of the Gate alive — dockhands, smugglers, a woman called Hessa down by the waterline. Go to the river, not your room. Say a stranger sent you. *Go now*, while the watcher's still unsure."

[PERSUASION] "The smiling man — Tallow — isn't sure of you yet. I can make him *un*-sure. Give me an hour and a good lie, and your name never reaches the Choir. Buy your apples. Walk home slow. Let me handle the watcher."

*(the hard truth, gently)* "I won't lie to you to make this easier. There may be no running far enough. But you have today, and you're *spending* it on sweetness and sun instead of terror, and that — Wren, that is the bravest use of a day I've seen in a long while. Don't let me take it from you with false hope."
"I'm sorry. I can't carry this for you. I have my own road, and it's a hard one." *(step back)*

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(She doesn't argue. She doesn't weep. She just *listens*, with the ferocious focus of the drowning given a rope, and repeats it back.)* The river. Not home. Hessa, by the waterline. A stranger sent me. *(she nods, once, hard.)* I can do that. I can *do* that. *(she presses three of the apples into your hands — sweet, red, absurd, the only payment she has.)* Take them. No — *take* them, I won't have you refuse, they're the best thing I own today and you've just given me a *tomorrow* to maybe own a few more. *(her eyes are bright and terrified and *alive.*)* I don't know your name. I'm going to tell Hessa it was an angel. She'll laugh. *(she's already moving, light on her feet for the first time.)* If I make it — if there's an after — I'll mourn you a stranger's mourning, the realest kind. *Thank you.* *(and she's gone toward the river, quick and quiet, a woman with a direction at last.)*

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(Hope is a dangerous thing to hand someone this hollowed out, and you watch her decide, against all her training, to take it.)* You'd do that. *(barely.)* Stand between me and the *watcher.* A stranger. *(she presses the heel of her hand to her mouth, steadies.)* All right. All right — apples, and home slow, and I let you work. *(fiercely, low.)* But if it goes wrong, you run too, you hear me? I'll not have a kindness get someone *else* a place on the Wall. *(she manages something almost like her real smile.)* Go on then. Go be clever at him. I'll be the dull woman buying apples who isn't worth a single line in his terrible little book. *(softer.)* I don't know your name. But I'll know it was you, by the watcher leaving. Go.

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(The refusal to lie to her lands harder than any comfort could, and something in her steadies because of it.)* ...The bravest use of a day. *(she tastes the words.)* No one's called me brave. Frightened, doomed, *unfortunate* — never brave. *(she looks down at the apple in her hand, and then, deliberately, takes a bite of it, juice on her chin, defiant.)* You're right. Damn you, you're *right*, and it's better than a lie. I have *today.* I have *this apple.* I have a stranger who told me the truth and called me brave for eating fruit in the sun. *(wet, fierce, smiling for real.)* That's more than most of the marked get. They get the smiling men and the lists. I got *you*, for an hour, telling me the truth gently. *(she squares her shoulders.)* I'm going to spend the rest of my days the way I spent this hour. Awake. Tasting things. Unafraid of the wrong things. *Thank you* — for not insulting me with hope you didn't have. It's the kindest thing anyone's done all week.

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(Relief and disappointment cross her face together — relief that you're not another smiling man with an angle, disappointment that you're not a rescue either.)* ...Right. Of course. *(the social smile, gentle now.)* Good apples, like you said. *(quietly, as you turn.)* Mind how you go. And — if you've a god, which I'd guess you might — say a word to them for the ones who haven't. *(she turns back to the barrel, a woman counting coppers and days.)* Someone should. It might as well be a stranger. They're the only ones with nothing to lose by it.

variants.text: *(The smile sharpens by a degree no one but you would ever notice, and his gaze settles on you the way a hand settles on a fresh page in a ledger.)* Well, now. *(pleasantly.)* A new face. An *interesting* face. *(he does not blink quite often enough.)* I do so like to know my neighbors, you understand. Their names. Their trades. Their *gods* — or, in some sad cases, their lack of one. *(softly, dreadfully friendly.)* You'll forgive an old man his little hobby. What god keeps *you*, friend?

*(His smile widens, conspiratorial, the warmth of one churchman recognizing another.)* Ah — a soul of the *faith.* Splendid. *(he leans in as to a colleague.)* Tell me, friend, do you not find this market simply *teeming* of late? So many faces. *(his eyes never stop moving over the crowd.)* And so many of them, when one *looks* closely... unaccounted for. No temple. No tithe. No god's good light. One does like to keep a *tally* of such things. For the Choir. Purely as a volunteer.

*(For just an instant the smile falters — even a man like this knows the Bone Lord's mark — and then it reassembles, oilier than before.)* My, my. You wear *that* openly. *(he doesn't reach for a guard; he reaches, mentally, for a page.)* How very bold. And how very *noteworthy.* I shall remember you, friend. I remember everyone. It's rather my whole purpose.

*(His smile, when it finds you, sharpens with genuine and dreadful *approval* — the recognition of a craftsman spotting good raw material.)* Ah. *You.* *(he straightens off the wall, almost warm.)* I've been hearing about *you* all morning, friend. A soul with a *practical* turn. No tiresome bleeding-heart fuss. *(he practically purrs.)* Do you know how *rare* that is, in a square full of people who go all soft over the marked? We should be *friends*, you and I. The Choir is always, always grateful for a steady hand. And steady hands are paid by the *head.*

*(The smile tightens by a degree; word has reached him that you're one of the *soft* ones, and soft is bad for the ledger.)* …Ah. The *kind* one. *(mildly, watching you the way one watches a leak in a roof.)* Yes, I've had several reports. Always *helping*, aren't you. Always *interfering.* *(he tilts his head, almost pitying.)* You should understand, friend, that kindness in a place like this isn't a virtue. It's a way of *volunteering* — your time, your coin, and eventually your *name* — for a great deal of trouble. *(he produces the little notebook.)* And the occasional mention. To the right people.

*(A pleasant, forgettable man in good plain clothes leans by the wall and watches the market the way some men watch a fire. He smiles to find you noticing him.)* Lovely crowd today. *(he does not introduce himself for a beat too long.)* You can learn a *great* deal about a place, you know, simply by *watching.* Who hurries. Who lingers. Who *prays* — and who only moves their lips and hopes no one's counting. *(the mild, dreadful smile.)* Tallow. Goodman Tallow. And *you* are…?

nodes.speaker: Goodman Tallow
nodes.text: *(He produces, from nowhere in particular, a small notebook, and does *not* write in it, which is somehow far worse than if he had.)* I'm a great friend of the Choir, you understand. A *volunteer.* I merely... notice things, and pass them along, and the good church is so *grateful.* *(his eyes drift, idly, toward an apple barrel across the square, where a young woman is smiling much too hard.)* That one, for instance. *(mildly.)* No god's light on her at all. Bound for the Wall, I shouldn't *wonder* — though one does like to be quite *certain* before one… mentions a name. It would be irresponsible otherwise. *(he turns the dead-pleasant smile back on you.)* You wouldn't happen to *know* her, would you? Help an old volunteer be *thorough.*

choices.text: [INSIGHT] "You're no volunteer. You're paid by the head — so much copper per godless soul you feed to the harvest. The Choir keeps its hands clean and you keep the difference. This isn't piety. It's piecework."

[RELIGION] "Mind your doctrine, Goodman. Kelemvor judges the dead — He did not appoint *you* to pre-sort the living for His convenience. What you do isn't devotion. It's murder with a notebook, and the Judge will weigh *that* ledger too, line by line."
[DECEPTION] "Her? Devout as a dawn bell. I've seen her at the Kelemvor shrine twice this tenday, weeping over a candle for her mother. You're wasting good ink on the faithful, Goodman."

[INTIMIDATION] "Close the book. Leave this market. And understand me, Goodman — if that woman's name ever reaches the Choir, there is exactly one volunteer I'll come looking for, and I will not be *thorough.* I'll be quick. Smile at *that.*"

*(let him feel the cold of the Wall come off you, close as breath)* "Here's a thing only the dead can tell you, Goodman. You've sold so much of yourself to the Choir there's almost nothing left inside — you're half-Faithless already and don't know it. The Wall is reaching for *you.* It's *close.* Keep your tally. Your name is near the top of it."
"I know her. She's nobody — marked, godless, not my problem. Do your thorough little duty, Goodman."
*(say nothing, hold his eyes a moment, and walk away)*

nodes.speaker: Goodman Tallow
nodes.text: *(The smile does not waver — that's the unnerving part — but something behind the eyes recalculates, fast.)* *Piecework.* *(he savors the word as though tasting a wine he didn't order.)* How *unkind.* And how... accurate. *(he tilts his head.)* Three coppers a confirmed soul, if you must know. Five, for a whole household. The Choir would never *dirty* itself with the arithmetic, of course — that's what men like me are *for.* The respectable need someone to do the noticing. *(he closes the notebook, but does not put it away.)* You see me clearly. I find I don't *mind* being seen clearly; so few bother. But understanding what I am, friend, and *stopping* what I am — *(the smile sharpens.)* —are very different verbs. Which one did you come over here to do?

nodes.speaker: Goodman Tallow
nodes.text: *(He waits, pleasant as a held knife, the notebook resting in his palm.)*

choices.text: [INTIMIDATION] "Stopping. Obviously. Close the book and leave, Goodman, while leaving is still a thing you get to do on your own legs."

[DECEPTION] "Understanding, only. I'm in the trade myself, two wards over — and *that* woman's already promised to my ledger. Poach her and you'll answer to my Choir contact, not yours. Move along, colleague."
"Neither, today. I just wanted to look the harvest in its real face for once. Now I have." *(walk away)*

nodes.speaker: Goodman Tallow
nodes.text: *(He weighs the lie. You watch him weigh it — the small recalculations, the cost-benefit of a soul he isn't *quite* certain of against the bother of a complication — and the arithmetic, this time, lands in your favor.)* …At the shrine. Weeping for her mother. *(he makes a small, regretful sound.)* How *tedious.* The genuinely devout are such poor business — all that praying, and not a copper in it for me. *(he draws a thin line through nothing in his book, a deletion of a name that was never written.)* Very well. I'll trouble the apple-woman no further; there are duller fish that swim with no god at all and *weep for no one.* *(he tips his hat, dreadful and courteous.)* A pleasure, friend. You've a quick tongue. Do be careful it doesn't get *itself* a line in someone's little book one day. *(and he drifts off into the crowd, hunting easier prey — and across the square, a young woman lets out a breath she's held for an hour.)*

nodes.speaker: Goodman Tallow
nodes.text: *(He listens to your lie with his head tilted, and at the end of it the smile *widens*, which is the worst thing it could do.)* …Twice at the shrine. Weeping for her mother. *(softly.)* You know, friend, I've stood in markets for *eleven years*, and I can tell you the single most reliable sign that a soul is godless and frightened? *(he leans in.)* Someone *vouches* for them. Suddenly. Warmly. To *me.* *(he opens the notebook and, for the first time, writes — two short words, unhurried.)* You've just told me she matters to *someone*, which means she's worth confirming, which means I'll send a man to her door tonight after all. *(he closes the book.)* Thank you for your *thoroughness*, friend. You've been more help than you intended. *(and the dread of it is total: you've handed him the very thing he was missing.)*

nodes.speaker: Goodman Tallow
nodes.text: *(✦ The lie does not merely convince him. It *transports* him. The details arrive unbidden and perfect — the candle she lights is for her mother *and* a brother lost at sea, the shrine-keeper knows her by name, she tithes in *winkles* because she's too poor for coin but too faithful to give nothing — and Goodman Tallow's pencil is *flying.*)* …winkles. *(he breathes, writing.)* Too poor for coin but too faithful to give *nothing.* Oh, that's — that's *exactly* the sort of detail my reports always lack. *(he looks up at you with damp, shining gratitude.)* Do you know, in eleven years no one has ever helped me *correct* the ledger before — only obstructed it. *(he draws a firm, final line through Wren's invisible name and writes, in careful capitals, CONFIRMED DEVOUT — EXEMPLARY.)* She's not just *off* the list now, friend. I've marked her *commended.* The Choir will leave that woman alone for *years.* *(he pockets the book, beaming.)* You've a *gift.* Truly. If you ever tire of honest work, do look me up. The harvest could use a liar of your *calibre* — *(a beat)* — to keep the *innocent* off the rolls, naturally. Naturally.

nodes.speaker: Goodman Tallow
nodes.text: *(💀 You commit. Gods, you *commit.* The lie starts well and then, somewhere around the third embellishment, it develops *ambition.* She weeps at the shrine for her mother — and her father — who was a *war hero* — beloved of *three separate gods* — and also a *Doomguide* — and possibly, you hear yourself say, *Kelemvor's own cousin* — )* *(Goodman Tallow's smile spreads across his face like spilled oil.)* …Kelemvor's *cousin.* *(he is delighted. He is writing very fast.)* The Judge of the Dead. Has a *cousin.* Who lives in the *Lower City.* And sells *winkles.* *(he looks up, radiant with cruel joy.)* Friend, I have heard a great many lies in eleven years, but never one so *generous* — you've not given me a *cover*, you've given me a *confession* with a *bow* on it. Nobody real has that many gods. *(he snaps the book shut.)* A man to her door within the hour. And do give my regards to the cousin of Kelemvor. *(the dread is total, and now it is also embarrassing.)*

nodes.speaker: Goodman Tallow
nodes.text: *(For the first time the smile thins to something almost human — the flicker of a man who has heard the precise words he spends every night not thinking.)* …Murder with a notebook. *(quietly.)* You quote the canon at me as though I haven't *read* it, friend. As though I don't *know.* *(he turns the little book over in his hands.)* I tell myself I only *notice.* That the men who come to the doors are the ones who *act*, and the Choir who *order* it, and the god who *permits* it — and that I am merely... the eye. A passive thing. An eye does no murder. *(his jaw works.)* You've a cruel gift, dropping the word *weigh* on a man like that. The Judge weighs the eye too, does He. *(he doesn't answer his own question.)* …Move along, friend. You've spoiled my afternoon, and I find I haven't the *stomach* for the apple-woman now. Small mercies. Take it and go, before I get my stomach back.

nodes.speaker: Goodman Tallow
