nodes.text: *(The pleasantness holds for one more second — and then, all at once, it *drains*, and what's left is a small, frightened, ordinary man who has always, always relied on no one pushing back.)* …I see. *(he closes the notebook with a soft, final snap and tucks it away as though it burns.)* You're one of the ones who *means* it. We do occasionally get one. *(he straightens his good plain coat with hands not entirely steady.)* I'll — yes. I'll find a different market. There are always other markets. *(he attempts the smile and it doesn't quite take.)* The woman's name never reaches the Choir, you have my word, for what a man like me's word is worth — which is *exactly* as much as the harm you've just promised me. That, I believe. *(he edges into the crowd, glancing back once.)* You've cost the harvest a soul today, friend. It won't notice. *I* will. Good day.

nodes.speaker: Goodman Tallow
nodes.text: *(He does not flinch. That's the thing about the small men of the machine: they have the whole weight of it at their backs, and they know it.)* *(softly, the smile gone steely.)* Threats. At a *volunteer of the Choir.* In a *public square.* *(he writes, slowly, deliberately, watching your face the whole time.)* Do you know what I notice *most* reliably, friend? The ones who *care* too loudly about a single marked soul. It's nearly always because they're hiding their *own* lack of a god behind all that noise. *(he closes the book.)* I've a name for the apple-woman now, and I've a *description* for you. Two coppers' work become five. *(he tips his hat, and the dread is total.)* Do give my regards to the Wall. I suspect you'll both be seeing rather more of it. *(and he melts into the crowd, unhurried, certain — and you have made it worse.)*

nodes.speaker: Goodman Tallow
nodes.text: *(The smile blooms into something almost *grateful*, and that is the most monstrous thing it has done yet.)* *Marked, godless, and not your problem.* *(he writes, swift and pleased.)* Oh, *well* said, friend. Well said. You've no idea how *rare* you are — most go all soft and noisy over the marked, all that *caring*, so tiresome. But you — *(he beams.)* You *understand.* The harvest isn't cruelty, it's *tidiness.* The world has only so much room, and the godless take up space they never paid a god to keep. *(he pockets the book, her fate in it.)* A man come to her door by morning. Clean. Quiet. *Thorough.* *(he tips his hat, and means it.)* Should you ever wish to *volunteer*, friend — a soul with your clarity — the Choir is always grateful, and I always need a second pair of eyes. Do think on it. *(and he drifts off, content, and across the square a young woman who smiled too hard is, though she doesn't yet know it, already a line in a ledger you wrote.)*

nodes.speaker: Goodman Tallow
nodes.text: *(The notebook lowers. For the first time the watching eyes stop moving over the crowd and fix, with a dawning, animal unease, entirely on you.)* …That's not — *(the smile is still there but it's nailed on now, a thing over a wound.)* You can't *know* that. The cold I— that's just the *draught*, off the river, everyone feels the— *(he stops, because you are looking at him the way he looks at *them*, and no one has ever turned that look on *him.*)* Half-Faithless. *(barely.)* I tithe. I attend. I *volunteer*, that's — that's the whole *point*, that's why I'm *safe*, I do the church's work so the church will— *(his hand drifts to his own chest, the way Wren's drifted to her marked wrist.)* …reaching for me. *(a whisper.)* You're lying. You're a godless trickster and you're *lying* to frighten an honest— *(but the certainty's gone out of him; you've put a crack in the one thing that let him sleep.)* …Go away. *Go away.* I've names to take. Important names. *(he doesn't take any. He stands very still, by the wall, feeling for a cold he'll never be sure isn't there now — and the apple-woman, unwatched, slips quietly home.)*

nodes.speaker: Goodman Tallow
nodes.text: *(He watches you turn away, and the smile follows you, patient and entirely unbothered.)* Off you go, then. *(pleasantly, to your back.)* No matter. I'm a *patient* man, friend — it's the whole of the craft. The ones who say nothing and walk away are simply the ones I haven't found the *name* for yet. *(he opens the little book.)* But I will. I always do. *(and as you go, you hear, soft and dreadful behind you, the scratch of a pencil beginning to describe a face that is yours.)*

variants.text: *(His head snaps toward you across the entire square as though you'd shouted his name.)* OH. *(he scrambles up, delighted and appalled at once.)* Oh, there's a *hole* where your god should be — I can see right *through* you to the cold. *(he cackles.)* The larder's got a room with your name on the door, friend, and the door's left *open.* Hee. *(he pats the fountain-rim.)* Sit. *Sit.* You and me, we're going to get along like a fucking house on fire.

*(He bows, mockingly, far too low, and nearly topples into the fountain.)* A servant of the *Judge.* *(he straightens, eyes glittering.)* Tell your master his Wall is *leaking*, priest. The screaming's getting *out.* I hear it at night, pressed up against the inside of my skull. *(slyly.)* Don't you? No. Course not. You grey bastards sleep *fine.* That's the whole problem with the lot of you — you sleep just *fine.*

*(He goes abruptly, horribly quiet — which is far worse than the raving.)* ...The old one's mark. *(softly, almost tender.)* He *built* the first one, you know. The Wall. Before your Kelemvor inherited the misery and slapped a kinder face on it. *(he tilts his head.)* You worship the *architect.* That's bold. That's stupid. *(a crooked grin.)* Round here, those are mostly the same fucking thing.

*(He cackles and claps his filthy hands.)* They cross the road from you, don't they! The good, god-fearing cowards. *(he spits into the fountain.)* I'd cross it *toward* you, friend. The Hells and the deep dark at least keep their *promises*, which is a damn sight more than the Wall ever did. Sit. Sit with the other thing they're frightened of.

*(He stops mid-rave and goes still, staring at you with naked, fellow-feeling recognition.)* ...Oh. *Oh.* You've got the cold in you too, haven't you. Not a *sliver* like mine — a whole *winter.* *(he scrambles closer, urgent.)* You've been carrying it all morning, off the dying, off the marked, off the Wall itself. I can *see* it sitting on you like frost on a grave. *(softly, almost tender.)* Sit. Sit with the only other soul in this square who's been *listening* as long as you have. We frozen few have to keep each other company.

*(He throws his head back and *cackles*, delighted, pointing at you across the fountain.)* OH! Oh, here's a *god-burner* if old Joss ever smelled one! *(he claps.)* You've been out there all morning kicking over altars, haven't you — I can see the soot of it on your soul. *(he pats the rim, gleeful.)* Sit, fellow blasphemer, *sit*, and help me curse the frightened bastards upstairs properly. Two heretics make a *choir*, you know. A choir the gods can't *stand.*

*(A wild-eyed soul in rags crouches by the fountain, muttering to the water — and then fixes on you with alarming, sudden focus.)* You. *Yes*, you, with the — *(he squints, and something in him sharpens.)* — oh, you've got the *look.* The road-look. The been-somewhere-and-*back* look. *(he pats the fountain-rim urgently.)* Sit. SIT. Old Joss has been waiting a long, cold while for someone who'd actually *hear* it.

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(He grips your sleeve, urgent, reeking of river-water and cheap wine.)* Listen. *Listen* to me. They tell you the Wall is *justice.* The godless *chose*, they say, chose the nothing, fair's fair. *(fiercely.)* Horseshit. I've *seen* it — in the water, in the wine, in the cracked back room of my own skull — the Wall isn't a *punishment*, friend, it's a *larder.* The gods are *frightened.* Frightened of the one day nobody needs them. And every Faithless soul they grind into that stone is one more *no* they'll never have to hear. *(he lets go, panting, and then — suddenly, terribly lucid — )* That's the truth nobody in this whole shitting market will say out loud. *(he fixes you.)* So. What've you got to say *back* to it? Go on. *Surprise* a madman.

choices.text: [ELF] "I have watched your frightened gods for three thousand years, prophet. You're not wrong — you're just young enough to still be *angry* about it. Give it an age or two. The anger goes cold and clear."
[TIEFLING] "A larder. *(you laugh)* You're telling a soul with literal Hell in her blood that the *gods* are the monsters? Friend, you're preaching to the fucking choir."
[DROW] "Down below, we built a whole civilization worshipping a goddess who eats us. Your sunlit gods only hide the hunger better. The Wall's the same old appetite in nicer robes."
[DWARF] "My folk carve names in stone so the dead are never forgotten. Your gods carve the dead *into* stone so they're forgotten forever. Same chisel, prophet. Opposite prayer."

[HALF-ORC] *(quietly)* "My mother was godless. They took her when I was small. I still remember the cold that came off the men who did it. You're not mad, prophet. You're just the only bastard in this square saying it out loud."

[GNOME] "Frightened gods — a testable hypothesis, actually. *(you tilt your head)* Hold still, prophet. I want to check the way the light bends around your raving. There may be something *measurable* under all that wine."
[HALFLING] "Big folk and big gods, always so terrified of being unneeded. We small ones never had the luxury of that fear, prophet. Nobody needs us — and look, still here, still eating well."

[WOMAN] *(you crouch to his level)* "Every woman in the Lower City already knows what it is to be ground down by something frightened of her saying *no.* You've just put the gods' name to it, prophet."

[MAN] "A larder. *(you sit heavily on the fountain-edge)* I've taken orders my whole life from frightened men who called it justice. You're telling me the gods are just my old sergeant, scaled up. Shit. That tracks. That tracks all the way down."
[KELEMVOR] "Watch your tongue about the Judge, old man. He took the Throne of Bones to make the dead's lot *kinder.* The Wall shames Him as much as it does you."

[FAITHLESS] "I serve no god, prophet — so I'm the one soul in this square the larder's actually *for.* And I think you're right, and I think it's coming for me, and I walked over here anyway. So tell me the rest. All of it."
[MYRKUL] *(let the cold of the Bone Lord answer for you)* "The architect's servant has no quarrel with the larder, prophet. We built it. We would build it again, and stock it deeper."
[TYMORA] "Lady Luck says the house always wins, prophet — and the gods built the house. But she *also* says the bold occasionally rob it blind. I like those odds a good deal better than yours."
[OGHMA] "An idea, once known, can never be unmade — that's my god's whole creed. So here's a known idea, prophet: the Wall is *afraid of being seen.* You've just seen it. It cannot un-happen now."
[LATHANDER] "The Morninglord says every night ends in a dawn. Even this one. Even the Wall. I *have* to believe that, prophet — don't you take it from me with your fountain and your wine."
[FIGHTER] "I'm a simple soul, prophet. I hit things that are wrong until they stop. Point me at a frightened god and let's find out what a sword does to a larder."

[WIZARD] "A larder needs a *preservative*, prophet — magic, then, old and divine, the kind that doesn't decay. You haven't described a moral structure. You've described an *arcane* one. And that, gods help us, is a thing that can be *unworked.*"

[CLERIC] "I channel a god's power every single day, prophet — and I have felt the exact fear you're describing on the *other end* of the prayer. They *are* afraid. I just never let myself say the word until you said it first."

[ROGUE] "Every con I've ever run worked because the mark was terrified of looking like a fool. You're telling me the gods are running the longest con in history off the very same fear. *(you grin)* Honestly? Respect."
[RANGER] "I track frightened things for a living, prophet. They all move the same way — lashing out, hoarding, walling themselves in. By that read, your gods are the most frightened creatures alive."
[ACOLYTE] "I took vows to a god, prophet, and quietly stopped believing them about a year ago. Hearing you bellow it at a fountain is the first honest sermon I've heard since."
[SOLDIER] "I've sacked towns for frightened lords who called it *strategy*, prophet. A frightened god calling a soul-larder *justice* is the same shit, bigger flag, fewer survivors."

[NOBLE] *(you adjust your cuffs)* "The powerful are always frightened of the powerless, prophet — it's the first lesson in any great house. Your gods are merely patriars with better real estate and a longer lease."
[CRIMINAL] "A protection racket — that's what you're describing. Pay a god, or we grind your soul to paste. I've run smaller versions out of an alley. The gods just have a much, much bigger crew."

[SAGE] "There's a heresy sealed in the Candlekeep stacks that says *precisely* this, prophet — the Wall as the gods' theological self-defense. They locked it away. You've reinvented it from a bottle and a fountain. That is genuinely remarkable."

[FOLK HERO] "Where I'm from, we've no patience for the frightened powerful telling us what's *just.* Sounds like the gods are overdue a folk hero. Shame all I've got is a strong back and a worse temper."

[CHARLATAN] "Finest con I ever heard, and I've heard a few — convince every soul alive that being *eaten* is *justice*, and get them thanking you for the temple on the way into the pot. The gods are artists, prophet. Wicked ones."

[LAWFUL] "If you're right, prophet, then the *law itself* is the crime — and a lawful soul has to choose between serving the law and serving the thing the law was meant to protect. You've thoroughly ruined my afternoon. Thank you."
[CHAOTIC] "Frightened gods and their precious bloody rules. *(you grin)* I've never met a rule I didn't want to piss on, prophet, and you've just handed me the biggest one in the cosmos. Cheers."

[GOOD] "If you're right, then somewhere a frightened god is grinding *kind* souls to dust to feel safe — and somebody ought to bloody stop them. Suppose that somebody's me now. Thanks for that, prophet. Truly."
[EVIL] *(you smile)* "A larder. How *efficient.* I don't object to the cruelty, prophet — only to the dishonesty of calling it justice. *Own* it, gods. That's all I ask of a decent evil."
[STRENGTH] *(you haul him gently up off the cold stone by the collar)* "Up. Off the freezing flags, prophet. Mad or not, you'll not catch your death on my watch while you've truth left to rave."

[INTELLIGENCE] "A larder implies a *finite* appetite, prophet. Feed it enough and it's full. You haven't described a doom — you've described a *quantity.* And quantities can be counted, and exhausted. That's almost hope."

[WISDOM] "You're not mad, Joss. You've seen something true and had no one believe you, for so long that the truth and the madness braided together to keep each other warm. I believe you. Does that help, even a little?"
[CHARISMA] *(you hold his wild gaze and do not look away)* "I hear you, Joss. Every word. And I am going to *do* something with it. Look at me. Look — do you believe that?"

[RELIGION] "The 'larder' has a *name* in the old sealed texts, prophet — the Hunger of the Throne. Heretical, buried, and almost word for word what you just raved at a fountain. *Where* in all the Hells did you read it?"

[INSIGHT] "You keep saying *I hear it at night.* That's not prophecy, Joss — that's *grief.* You lost someone to the Wall, didn't you. The vision is just the only shape big enough to carry it. Who was it?"

[ARCANA] "A preservative structure that size would warp the Weave for *leagues* — and prophet, it *does.* Mages have charted a dead-magic zone round the Wall for centuries and never once asked *why.* You did. Drunk, at a fountain. You asked."

[HERETICAL] "You and I have been burning gods all day, prophet. Here's the last log for the fire: a god you can *shame* isn't a god at all. And a larder — *(you lean in)* — a larder can be *emptied.*"

[HAUNTED] *(quietly)* "I've been carrying the cold all morning too, Joss. Off the others. Off the Wall. You and I might be the only two souls in this whole bright square who can feel it. That's a lonely fucking club."

[MERCIFUL] *(you press your last coppers and a heel of bread into his hands)* "Eat first, prophet. The prophecy keeps. Hunger doesn't. Tell me the rest once there's something in you besides wine and the cold."

*(you show him the grey holding-stone the charm-seller gave you)* "A woman here told me the one thing the nothing can't eat is a soul that's *remembered.* Is that your larder's single flaw, prophet? Witnesses? Is *that* why the gods hate to be seen?"
"There's a woman at the apple barrel, Joss — marked, godless, days left. Your 'larder' isn't theory to her. Is there *anything* in your visions that helps her, or is it only horror all the way down?"

"There's a young Doomguide across the square starting to think exactly what you're raving, prophet. The Wall's losing its own keepers, one conscience at a time. Maybe your screaming *is* getting out after all."

*(you let the Wall's cold pour off you until the very fountain seems to skin with frost)* "You want to know what's *in* the larder, Joss? I'll tell you. I've *been* in it. I went into the stone, and something dragged me back out with the cold still on me. You're not mad. You're the only living soul who can hear the thing I can't stop hearing."

[PERSUASION] "Joss. Joss, look at me. You've carried this alone too long, and it's eating you faster than any wine. Come with me — there are people who actually *fight* the Wall. You don't have to just sit here and *watch* it leak."

[INTIMIDATION] "Lower your voice, prophet. *Now.* You're bellowing things that get the godless poor a one-way walk to the very Wall you're raving about. Shut your mouth, for the sake of every neck in earshot."

[DECEPTION] *(soothing, false)* "Of course, Joss — the larder, the frightened gods, I've seen it all too. *(you haven't)* Tell me where you go at night to hear the screaming. I'll come and listen with you. We'll bear it together."
"It's a fountain, and you're drunk, friend. But here — *(a copper)* — get yourself somewhere warm before the night does for you."
"I haven't time for prophecy today, Joss. The Wall will still be leaking tomorrow." *(move on)*

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(He seizes both your hands in his filthy ones, and for one second the madness drains entirely out of his eyes, leaving something unbearably clear.)* You — you *heard* it. *(a whisper, then a crow of joy.)* You actually fucking *heard* it! Thirty years I've shouted it at the water and the wine and the backs of running people, and you're the *first* — *(he laughs, wild and wet.)* Then hear the last of it, the part I never get to, because they're always gone by now: a larder can be *emptied*, friend. A god that *feeds* can be *starved.* Every soul you pull out of the harvest, every name you make them *witness*, every god you make *flinch* — that's a meal it doesn't get. *(he grips harder.)* They built the Wall because they're afraid of *no.* So be the loudest *no* the cosmos ever heard. *(he releases you, beaming, ruined, free.)* Go on. Go be unbearable to a frightened god. Old Joss'll be right here, raving you on.

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(He recoils, then barks a laugh, delighted by your coldness in the way only the truly mad can be.)* Oh, you're a *nasty* one. *(he wags a finger.)* No horror, no pity, just — *yes, it's a larder, so what.* *(he cackles.)* You know what, friend? That's almost *honest.* Crueller than the priests and twice as *truthful.* *(he leans in, conspiratorial.)* But here's what the cold ones always miss: a larder doesn't care if you *approve* of it. It's still going to eat you too, one cold day, no matter how much you *admire* the carpentry. *(he settles back against the fountain.)* Go on, then. Go be efficient at the world. *(softly, to the water, as you turn.)* They always think being on the knife's side keeps them off the bread. It never does. Never once.

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(The kindness lands somewhere the raving can't protect, and all at once he is just an old man on cold stone, and his eyes fill.)* ...You — *(he looks at the copper, the bread, your hand, as if he's forgotten what gentleness is shaped like.)* People don't. People give me *coins* to go *away*, friend, never to make me *warm.* *(he scrubs his face with a ragged sleeve.)* I'm not mad, you know. Not all the way. *(quietly, lucid, terrible.)* I had a daughter. Godless, like her mother. The harvest took her four winters back, and I went down to the Wall to scream at it, and I *heard* her in the stone, friend, I *heard her*, and I have not been able to *stop* hearing her since. *(a wet, broken smile.)* That's the prophecy. That's all it ever was. A father who can't put his girl down. *(he closes his hands around the bread.)* Thank you. For the warm thing. For not telling me she's at peace. She isn't. But you knew that. You've got the road-look. You *know.*

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(He studies you, and the mockery gentles into something almost like respect.)* Defending the grey, eh. *(he nods slowly.)* The *kind* version. Kelemvor-wants-it-better, the Morning-comes, the law-will-be-redeemed. *(he sighs, not unkindly.)* I used to wear that coat too, friend, before the cold got in. And maybe you're right. Maybe there's a god up there *ashamed* of the larder, wringing His holy hands. *(he leans close.)* But ask yourself this, and then go: a god who's *ashamed* of the Wall and *keeps* it anyway — is that better than a god who's *proud* of it? Or just *worse*, for knowing? *(he settles back.)* Go serve your kind, ashamed god. Truly. Someone should. Just — don't let the shame become the *excuse.* That's the trap the grey ones always fall in. *Knowing it's wrong, and standing the watch anyway.*

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(He goes still — properly still — and stares at you with a dreadful new sobriety, as though you'd answered a riddle he thought was his alone.)* ...You *know* it. *(a whisper.)* Not believe. Not pity. *Know.* The name, the Weave, the sealed page — you've *read* what I only *heard.* *(he grips the fountain-rim.)* Then I'm not mad, am I. *(a strange, cracked laugh of pure relief.)* Thirty years I thought the wine had rotted a hole in my head and the screaming came *through* it. But you — sober, lettered, *clear* — you've come to the same black place by the front door. *(fiercely, lucid.)* Then it's *real*, and it's *written*, and a thing that's written can be *read aloud*, and a thing read aloud can't be sealed again. *(he seizes your sleeve.)* You carry that, friend. You're a *literate* madman, the most dangerous kind. Go and make the gods *read.*

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(Every scrap of performance falls away. What's left is small, and old, and unbearably sane.)* ...You saw it. Under the raving. *(he nods, slowly.)* A daughter. Godless, like her mother before her. The harvest took her four winters past, and I went down to that Wall to *scream* at it — and I *heard* her, friend. In the stone. I *heard her*, and I have not stopped hearing her one single night since. *(his voice doesn't break; it's worn far past breaking.)* So I 'prophesy.' I dress my girl's voice up as the doom of the gods, because a *prophet* people only step around — but a *grieving father* they'd lock away, and then who'd listen for her? *(he meets your eyes.)* You asked who I lost. No one's *asked*, in four years. They argue the theology or they give me a coin. You *asked.* *(a long breath.)* Her name was Mira. Say it sometime, would you, where the cold can hear. She'd like that someone living knew it. Mira.

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(The cold rolls off you and hits him, and he does not flinch — he *inhales* it, like a drowning man breaking the surface. Then, slowly, in the middle of the bright crowded square, Mad Joss gets to his knees.)* ...It's *you.* *(a whisper with the whole of him behind it.)* The one who comes *back.* I've raved it for years and they laughed — *the larder has a door, the larder has a door* — and here you *are*, with the door still *open* behind your eyes. *(he's weeping and grinning at once.)* You walked *into* the stone. You heard them — you heard *her*, didn't you, you heard *all* of them — and the nothing reached for you and you walked *back out.* *(he seizes your hands, reverent, terrified.)* Then it *can* be done. A soul *can* come back from the larder. Everything I screamed at the water is *true*, and *you are the proof of it walking.* *(fierce, transformed.)* Go, Returned. Go to the Wall and leave the fucking door open behind you. I'll be here, telling everyone who'll listen and everyone who won't: *I met the one who came back. The larder is not forever. I met the one who came back.*

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(He stops mid-sway, and for the first time the wild eyes *focus* on a future instead of a horror.)* People who *fight* it. *(slowly, as if the words are in a language he'd forgotten.)* Not argue it. Not *bless* it. *Fight* it. *(he wipes his mouth.)* I'd half-decided the only thing left to do with the truth was *shout* it until the cold took me too. *(he looks at your outstretched hand.)* But you're saying there's *work.* That a cracked old man who can hear the inside of the Wall might be *useful* to someone, instead of just *loud.* *(he takes your hand, and stands, unsteady, taller.)* All right. *All right.* Take me to your soul-fighters, Returned. Let's go be a *nuisance* to a frightened god. *(a crooked, watery grin.)* Mira always said I'd find a better use for my mouth eventually. Took me four winters and a stranger, but here we fucking are.

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(He shakes his head, gently, and pats your hand off his sleeve like a man declining a kindness he can't afford.)* No, no. *(almost tender.)* You're good to ask, friend, you truly are. But I can't *leave* the fountain. *(he glances at the water, and you understand.)* This is where I can hear her clearest. The water carries it up from the river, and the river runs past the Wall. *(quietly.)* If I go and *fight* — if I'm useful and busy and warm — I'll stop *listening*, and then who keeps her company in the cold? *(he settles back onto the stone.)* No. You go and fight it. That's *your* road, I can see it on you. *Mine's* to sit right here and make sure that as long as one soul's screaming in that stone, one soul out here is *still answering.* *(he turns back to the water.)* Go on. And — leave the door open. For her. If you can.

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(The intimidation lands, and the wild light in him dims to something wary and old. He lowers his voice to a rasp.)* ...All right. *All right.* You're not wrong, damn you. *(he hunches smaller against the fountain.)* The smiling men *do* listen. Tallow and his little book. I've watched him walk the godless poor right onto the lists while they stood there buying *apples.* *(bitterly.)* So I should *whisper* the truth, should I, to keep necks off the Wall. *(he laughs, mirthless.)* That's the gods' whole trick, friend — make the truth *dangerous to say*, and the silence does the rest. *(he eyes you.)* But you're right that the dead don't need me *hanged* alongside them. *(grudging.)* I'll keep it to the water from now on. The water doesn't inform. *(quietly, as you go.)* You frightened me quiet to *save* the poor, or to be *rid* of me? ...No. Don't tell me. Let me keep the kinder guess.

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(He does not quiet. If anything he swells, and his cracked voice rings off the fountain so the whole square half-turns.)* SHUT IT? *(delighted, furious.)* You sound just like *them*, friend! *Keep your voice down, Joss, mind your betters, Joss, don't say the frightened-god thing where the help can hear it!* *(he flings his arms wide.)* That's how a larder STAYS full — everyone politely *whispering* while it eats! *(he points a filthy finger at you.)* You want me quiet, you'll have to do what *they* do and put me in the *stone* — and even then, friend, EVEN THEN, you'll hear me through it on the cold nights, same as I hear my Mira! *(he cackles as you step back.)* Go on! Run from the loud old man! Everybody does! It's the running that keeps the gods *fed!*

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(He believes you. That's the unbearable part — the wild face opens up with such naked, grateful hope that the lie curdles in your mouth even as it works.)* You'll *come?* *(barely.)* To the listening-place? With *me?* *(he clutches your sleeve.)* No one's ever — they all say *of course, Joss, of course*, and then they're gone by morning and I'm at the water *alone* again. *(he searches your face, and finds the false warmth, and takes it for true because he needs it so.)* But you mean it. You've the road-look, you'd *hear* her too — *(he names a place, a drain-mouth down by the river where the cold comes up, and you file it away for nothing, for a lie, for the small clean cruelty of having gotten what you wanted.)* Tonight, then. After dark. *(he beams, ruined and radiant.)* I'll save you the dry stone to sit on. Oh, friend. *Friend.* Four winters, and finally someone's coming to listen with me.

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(He goes very still, and then he *laughs* — soft, knowing, infinitely sad.)* Oh, that's a *practised* warmth, that is. *(he pats your hand, almost fond.)* *I've seen it too*, you say, all gentle, all leaning-in — and your eyes the whole time flat as a frozen pond. *(he shakes his head.)* You haven't heard a thing, friend. You just wanted my listening-place, gods know why — somewhere quiet to do a quiet crime, most like. *(he turns back to the water, dismissing you.)* I'll keep it to myself, then. The cold tells *me* its secrets because I never once lied to *it.* That's the whole trick you'll never learn. *(quietly.)* Off you go. And mind — a soul that practises warmth it doesn't feel is already half-Faithless, whatever god it claims. The larder *likes* the hollow ones. Less to chew.

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(✦ You don't just persuade him. You *ignite* him. Whatever you say lands in the dry tinder of thirty years of unheard prophecy and goes up like a fucking *cathedral.* Mad Joss rises from the fountain not as a beggar but as a man who has just been told his entire life was *correct.*)* People who *fight* it. *(his voice cracks the air; the whole square turns.)* PEOPLE — WHO — FIGHT — IT! *(he seizes you by both shoulders, weeping, radiant, terrifying.)* You're no traveler. You're an *ANGEL.* You're the *answer to the water!* THIRTY YEARS and the cold finally sent me— *(he spins to the crowd, arms wide.)* HEAR ME! THIS ONE HERE HAS COME TO FIGHT THE LARDER! THE WALL CAN BE EMPTIED! I'VE SAID IT AND SAID IT AND NOW HEAVEN HAS SENT A SOLDIER— *(a fishmonger flees. A pigeon applauds, somehow.)* *(he wheels back to you, gripping your hands.)* I'm yours. Body, mouth, and madness, I am *yours.* Where do we *march?* Old Joss is going to be the most *useful* lunatic a rebellion ever fielded. Mira — *Mira*, do you hear, in the cold — your old da found the *door!*

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(💀 You reach for inspiring and you *miss.* Some word in your rousing speech — 'come with me,' perhaps, or 'leave the fountain,' or simply the earnest, reasonable *warmth* of it — trips a wire deep in the madman's wiring, and you watch, helpless, as his face slams shut like a cellar door.)* …'Come with you.' *(he scrambles backward off the fountain-rim.)* Away from the water. Away from where I can *hear* her. *(his eyes go wide and knowing and entirely, catastrophically wrong.)* Oh. *Ohhh.* You're one of *THEM*, aren't you. The frightened gods sent you. *Smiling*, *reasonable*, *come away from the listening-place, Joss, come somewhere nice and warm and quiet where you'll stop HEARING THE EVIDENCE—* *(he points a shaking finger, backing into the crowd.)* I'll not be *recruited* off my post by a god's pretty errand-boy! NICE TRY! *(to a passing stranger, urgently)* That one's an *agent!* Look at its *reasonable face!* *(and you have, somehow, by being kind, recruited yourself a sworn and very loud enemy.)*

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(✦ The lie is so good that *you* half-believe it. You lean in, you lower your voice to the exact pitch of shared grief, you say *I hear it too* — and something uncanny happens: for one disorienting second, in the hush by the fountain, you *almost do.* Joss's whole ruined face opens like a sunrise.)* You *hear* her. *(a whisper of pure, devastating joy.)* You said *her.* I never — I never said it was a *her*, friend, I only said *the screaming*, but *you* said *her*, which means you've *heard* her, you've heard my *Mira* — *(he's weeping, clutching you, and the lie curdles to acid in your mouth because it has worked far, far too well.)* Tonight. The drain-mouth by the river, where the cold comes up. We'll listen *together*, you and I, two souls who *hear* — *(he presses his filthy forehead to your knuckles, overcome.)* Four winters alone in the cold, and the gods finally sent me *company.* *(and you have what you wanted, whatever it was, and the having of it feels like the worst thing you have ever done — which, for a critical *success*, is its own kind of perfect.)*

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(💀 You go for sincere and land somewhere near *amateur dinner-theatre.* 'I've seen it too,' you say, pressing a hand to your heart a half-beat too late, and then — fatally — you try to *match a detail*, and you guess *wrong.*)* …the screaming. *(you nod gravely.)* From the — the souls. In the — the *fire.* *(Joss stops. Joss tilts his head.)* …Fire. *(flatly.)* The Wall's a *larder*, friend. It's *cold.* It's the *coldest* thing there is. *(the wild eyes narrow to slivers.)* There's no fire in it. There's never been any *fire* in it. *(he stands, and for once he is not the mad one in this conversation.)* You've not heard a *thing.* You're a *tourist.* A grief-tourist, come to *try on* my dead like a hat in a shop. *(genuinely, witheringly disgusted.)* Get away from my fountain. The cold doesn't *perform* for sightseers, and neither do I. *(he turns his back, and it is the most lucid, most cutting thing anyone has said to you all day.)*

nodes.speaker: Mad Joss
nodes.text: *(He nods, unsurprised, and turns gently back to his conversation with the water.)* Tomorrow, aye. *(softly.)* The Wall'll be leaking tomorrow, and the day after, and every day until someone with the road-look stops *walking past* and starts walking *in.* *(he trails his fingers in the fountain.)* That's all right, friend. Old Joss is patient as the cold. *(as you go, you hear him resume, low and ceaseless, to the water:)* ...a larder, do you hear me, a *larder*, and they call it justice, and the screaming's getting *out*...
